description: The Qutub Minar, located in Delhi, is the world's tallest brick minaret, standing at 73 meters high. Built by Qutb-ud-din Aibak in 1193 to mark the beginning of Muslim rule, it serves as a victory tower and is a UNESCO World Heritage site. This five-storeyed structure features intricate carvings in red sandstone and marble, showcasing early Indo-Islamic architecture.
tagline: The Tower of Triumph—Where History Soars

description: The Gateway of India is an iconic 26-meter-high basalt arch monument located on the waterfront at Apollo Bunder in South Mumbai. Completed in 1924, it was built to commemorate the visit of King George V and Queen Mary to India in 1911. Designed in the Indo-Saracenic style, it served as a symbolic ceremonial entrance to India for British Viceroys.
tagline: The Majestic Archway: Where History Meets the Sea

description: The Taj Mahal is an ivory-white marble mausoleum located in Agra, India, commissioned in 1632 by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan for his favorite wife, Mumtaz Mahal. Built over 20 years, it features a central dome, intricate inlay work, and vast gardens, serving as an ultimate monument to love.
tagline: A Timeless Teardrop on the Cheek of Time

description: Jag Mandir, also known as the Lake Garden Palace, is a stunning 17th-century palace built on an island in Udaipur's Lake Pichola. Commissioned by the Maharana of Mewar, it served as a summer retreat and famously provided refuge to Prince Khurram (later Emperor Shah Jahan).
tagline: Where Royal Dreams Float on Quiet Waters

description: The Golden Temple, or Sri Harmandir Sahib, located in Amritsar, Punjab, is the pre-eminent spiritual site of Sikhism, revered for its stunning gold-plated architecture and serene ambience. Founded in the 16th century by Guru Ram Das and completed by Guru Arjan, it is built on a lower level to promote humility and features four entrances to symbolize equality for all people, regardless of faith or background.
tagline: Where Golden Splendor Meets Eternal Serenity

description: Located in New Delhi, the India Gate is a 42-meter-high national war memorial designed by Sir Edwin Lutyens and inaugurated in 1931. It honours nearly 70,000 Indian soldiers who sacrificed their lives for the British Army during World War I and the Afghan War.
tagline: A Timeless Tribute to Eternal Valor
